parent_statement: Near-Term Climate Action Priorities – Transportation: For-hire fleet electrification
policy_statement: Work with community and business stakeholders to develop recommendations for making all new forhire vehicles in Seattle electric.
verbatim_text: Work with community and business stakeholders to develop recommendations for making all new forhire vehicles in Seattle electric. The way people move themselves and goods around Seattle is changing rapidly, and as we continue to advance the percentage of trips taken by transit, bicycle, or walking, we must ensure that new mobility services, such as for hire vehicles, use clean energy to power their trips.
has_timeline: Yes — timeline to determine a year after which all for-hire vehicles must be electric (to be determined)
extraction_rationale: Policy to study and set future electric-for-hire requirements.
rejection_reason: The statement centers on process (“work with stakeholders” to “develop recommendations”) rather than committing to an outcome. It lacks a quantifiable target (no date, percentage, or mandate in effect) and has no binding mechanism (no law/regulation). While it implies a technological shift (all new for-hire vehicles electric) and references a sector and city in the text, there is no enforceable commitment or defined deadline—only an intent to identify one later. This fits “process without outcome” and “aspirational language without a roadmap,” making it non-actionable at present.
weak_signals: work with stakeholders, develop recommendations, to be determined, ensure, clean energy, advance the percentage
strong_signals: all new vehicles, electric, for-hire vehicles, sector specificity, Seattle (location), technological shift

parent_statement: Near-Term Climate Action Priorities – Buildings: Oil to heat pump conversion
policy_statement: Propose recommendations to mayor to convert 18,000 homes from heating oil to an electric heat pump.
verbatim_text: Propose recommendations to mayor to convert 18,000 homes from heating oil to an electric heat pump. OSE will propose recommendations to the mayor aimed at accelerating the transition of 18,000 homes from heating with oil to an electric heat pump, and adequately serving those unable to finance the switch alone.
extraction_rationale: Targeted program proposal with a concrete number.
rejection_reason:
- Contains quantifiable figure (18,000 homes), a clear technological shift (oil to electric heat pumps), and city-wide scope. These are strong markers.
- However, the operative action is only to "propose recommendations" to the mayor—this is a process step without a commitment to implement conversions or achieve the target. 
- No timeline, no binding or regulatory mechanism, and no delivery plan or funding commitment. Language like “aimed at accelerating” and “adequately serving” is aspirational/vague.
- Net: Despite numeric scope and clear technology, the policy itself is not actionable/measurable; it is administrative/performative.
weak_signals: propose; recommendations; aimed at; accelerate; adequately serving; unable to finance; no timeline; no enforcement
strong_signals: 18,000 homes; convert/transition; from heating oil to electric heat pump; city-wide scope

parent_statement: Near-Term Climate Action Priorities – Transportation: New Mobility Playbook
policy_statement: Adopt the 2017 New Mobility Playbook to guide Seattle's transportation future toward shared, electric, and autonomous mobility.
verbatim_text: New Mobility Playbook. How we get around is undergoing a sea change, and Seattle is preparing for a transportation future that is shared, electric, and autonomous. As transportation becomes increasingly shared, active, self-driving, electric, and data-driven, Seattle is planning ahead to ensure the fast-paced changes in mobility help us create a safe, equitable, sustainable city serving our diverse needs. The 2017 New Mobility Playbook sets out a course for how Seattle will ensure that future of transportation in Seattle aligns with our city's goals and values and will position Seattle to encourage and guide innovation in transportation technology, reorganizing our streets to grow healthy communities and vibrant public spaces.
extraction_rationale: Strategic guidance document for mobility future.
rejection_reason: The policy adopts a guidance document without specifying measurable outcomes. It lacks quantifiable targets, no timeline/deadline, and no binding mechanism. While it is spatially specific to Seattle and references a technological shift (shared/electric/autonomous), the language is largely aspirational and process-oriented (“preparing,” “planning ahead,” “guide,” “ensure,” “align with goals and values,” “encourage/position”). Per criteria, the presence of spatial scope and tech themes is outweighed by the absence of concrete, enforceable, or measurable commitments; thus it reads as guidance rather than actionable policy.
weak_signals: preparing; planning ahead; guide; ensure; align with goals and values; encourage; position; future; playbook; sets out a course
strong_signals: Seattle (city-wide scope); Adopt the 2017 New Mobility Playbook (named policy); references shift to shared, electric, autonomous mobility
